core_meaning: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is a chameleon word with two main faces: as a な-adjective or adverb it means 'quite / pretty / fairly' (often with a tinge of surprise that something is more X than expected), and as a standalone response it means 'No thank you' or 'That's fine as-is' — a polite refusal or signal that nothing more is needed.

sentences.jp: このラーメン、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>辛<rt>から</rt></ruby>いね。
sentences.translation: This ramen is pretty spicy, huh.
sentences.notes: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> as an adverb meaning 'quite/fairly' — the nuance is mild surprise that it's spicier than expected.

sentences.jp: 「もう<ruby>少<rt>すこ</rt></ruby>しいかがですか？」「いいえ、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です。ありがとうございます。」
sentences.translation: 'Would you like a little more?' 'No, I'm fine, thank you.'
sentences.notes: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です as a polite refusal — one of its most important uses and one learners often miss.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かったよ。
sentences.translation: That movie was actually pretty interesting.
sentences.notes: The 'actually more than I expected' undertone of <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> comes through here — the speaker is a little surprised.

sentences.jp: <ruby>駅<rt>えき</rt></ruby>から<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>歩<rt>ある</rt></ruby>くことになったんだけど、まあいい<ruby>運動<rt>うんどう</rt></ruby>になったかな。
sentences.translation: We ended up walking quite a bit from the station, but I guess it was good exercise.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> modifying a verb phrase — 'quite a lot' rather than modifying a single adjective.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>は<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>大変<rt>たいへん</rt></ruby>ですが、やりがいがあります。
sentences.translation: This work is quite demanding, but it's rewarding.
sentences.notes: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> in a standard register, tempering the statement — not 'extremely hard' but 'harder than you might think.'

dont_use: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です as a refusal only works in polite contexts — saying it too curtly to a close friend can sound oddly stiff; in casual settings just say いらない or いい. Also, don't confuse the adverb meaning with かなり or とても: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> implies 'more than expected' and is softer than かなり, so swapping them can over- or under-state your meaning. Avoid using <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です as acceptance ('That's great!') — its most natural reading as a full response is refusal, which causes embarrassing misunderstandings.

confused_with.contrast: A learner might say かなり<ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かった when they really just mean 'it was pretty good, more than I thought' — but かなり means 'considerably / quite seriously' and implies a stronger degree without the surprise nuance. <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かった sounds natural for mild pleasant surprise, while かなり<ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かった suggests it was genuinely very interesting. Use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> when you want to say 'more than I expected but not extreme'; use かなり when the degree itself is the point.

confusion_set.use_when: The degree is higher than expected but not extreme, OR you want to politely decline something.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>値段<rt>ねだん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>かったよ。（More expensive than I thought.）

confusion_set.use_when: The degree is objectively considerable — no surprise element needed, just emphasis on extent.
confusion_set.example: かなり<ruby>疲<rt>つか</rt></ruby>れたから、もう<ruby>寝<rt>ね</rt></ruby>る。（I'm considerably tired, so I'm going to sleep.）

confusion_set.use_when: Something is relatively/comparatively X — lighter and more colloquial than <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>, often meaning 'for what it is.'
confusion_set.example: この<ruby>店<rt>みせ</rt></ruby>、わりと<ruby>安<rt>やす</rt></ruby>いね。（This place is relatively cheap, isn't it.）

frequency: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is very common in everyday spoken Japanese across all age groups. The adverb meaning appears constantly in casual and standard speech. The refusal meaning (<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です) is standard in polite/service interactions. Both senses are essential for natural conversation.
anki_hint: Remember <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> has two jobs: as an adverb it means 'quite/pretty (more than expected),' and as a response <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です is a polite 'No thank you' — don't mix them up when someone offers you something!
